coredata.dc:title: 3D printed micro-structured hydrogel device with transmittance-changeable soft ‘armor’ camouflage for dual-encryption
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Jia-Yue
Lv, Tian-Run
Xue, Yi-Han
Zhang, Wen-Hai
Yin, Ming-Jie
An, Quan-Fu
coredata.dc:description: Hydrogel-based physical dual-encryption typically relies on the reversible shape-deformation of polymer chains for the decryption. However, the stored information is confronted by indecipherability at the microscale because the intrinsic relaxation of polymer chains would disrupt the fidelity of encoded information. Inspired by female Doryteuthis opalescens squid’s camouflage strategy (i.e., switching a stripe on its mantle from nearly transparent to opaque white), we devised a reversible transmittance-changeable hydrogel layer to fully cover the mono-encrypted information layer for dual-encryption. The micro-structured hydrogel was manufactured via 3D printing to encode the information, which can be decoded by UV-induced fluorescent emission. Then, the white camouflage hydrogel was constructed to conceal the mono-encrypted layer, which will not affect the information encoding layer during the reversible polymer chain mobility. The information can be decoded with alkaline solution and UV irradiation in a rapid speed, i.e., in 4 min, with good reusability. Moreover, the information was accurately unveiled even at microscale resolution, holding great promise for high-level encryption at the device level.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dual encryption
3D printing
Hydrogel device
Hydrogen bond
Carbon dot

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The design and fabrication of dual-encryption hydrogel
Characterization and test of the dual-encryption hydrogel device
Mechanism of pH-triggered phase separation and recovery

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22278010, 22125801).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The principle of the female Doryteuthis opalescens squid camouflages itself. (b) Illustration of the mechanism for the pH-controlled order change of polymer chains to mimic the female Doryteuthis opalescens squid camouflage process: the ordered polymer chains (transparent hydrogel layer, pH > 12) changes into aggregated polymer chains (opaque layer, pH ≤ 12). (c) Scheme for the fabrication process of reversible decryption-encryption hydrogel device.

The SEM image (a) and laser scanning confocal image (b) of the 3D printed CDs@PAA QR code. (c) Microscope picture of the QR code and the measured linewidth. (d) The decryption and encryption process of the mono-encryption hydrogel device, scale bar: 2 mm.

(a) The decryption and encryption process of the dual-encryption hydrogel device by the combination of pH and UV irradiation. (b) Multiple cycles reversible transmittance variation of PAA/PVP hydrogel treated with pH = 0 and pH = 14 solution, alternatively. The dynamic transmittance changes of the PAA/PVP hydrogel after treatment with strong alkaline (c) and strong acids (d). Scale bar: 2 mm.

(a) Schematic illustration of pH-induced reversible phase separation via hydrogen bond formation-dissociation of the PAA/PVP hydrogel. (b) Cross-sectional morphology of the PAA/PVP hydrogel treated with pH = 0 solution (c) and pH = 14 solution (d). FTIR spectra (e), LF-NMR spectra (f), and DSC curves.

head.ce:title: 3D printed micro-structured hydrogel device with transmittance-changeable soft ‘armor’ camouflage for dual-encryption

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Beijing Key Laboratory for Green Catalysis and Separation, Department of Chemical Engineering, College of Materials Science and Engineering, Beijing University of Technology, Beijing 100124, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Beijing Key Laboratory for Green Catalysis and Separation
College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Beijing Key Laboratory for Green Catalysis and Separation, Department of Chemical Engineering, Beijing University of Technology, Beijing 100124, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hydrogel-based physical dual-encryption typically relies on the reversible shape-deformation of polymer chains for the decryption. However, the stored information is confronted by indecipherability at the microscale because the intrinsic relaxation of polymer chains would disrupt the fidelity of encoded information. Inspired by female Doryteuthis opalescens squid’s camouflage strategy (i.e., switching a stripe on its mantle from nearly transparent to opaque white), we devised a reversible transmittance-changeable hydrogel layer to fully cover the mono-encrypted information layer for dual-encryption. The micro-structured hydrogel was manufactured via 3D printing to encode the information, which can be decoded by UV-induced fluorescent emission. Then, the white camouflage hydrogel was constructed to conceal the mono-encrypted layer, which will not affect the information encoding layer during the reversible polymer chain mobility. The information can be decoded with alkaline solution and UV irradiation in a rapid speed, i.e., in 4 min, with good reusability. Moreover, the information was accurately unveiled even at microscale resolution, holding great promise for high-level encryption at the device level.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Encryption is essential for safely information transmission in the current breakneck-speed developed information age, which is closely relevant to the human health, social stability, as well as national safety [1–4]. It is estimated that the market of the data encryption industry will grow from USD 13.4 billion in 2022 to USD 38.5 billion by 2030 [5]. Physical encryption realizes information encryption by changing the feature of physical patterns, i.e., encoded information, which possesses superior crypticity compared with digital encryption [6,7]. In this regard, physical encryption has deserved intensive research attention, in virtue of its diverse encryption-decryption paths that can be tailored to meet the safe information delivery demand basically [4,8–11]. Typically, the physical encryption relies on a single stimulus (e.g., light, pH, solvents, temperature, and ions) to unlock the information, which is insufficient to ensure a high safety index [12]. Dual-encryption calls for two consecutive steps to unveil the locked information, which potentially improves the safety level [7,13,14]. To this end, advanced materials with good microfabrication ability (encoding information) and reversible stimulus-responsive capability (encryption and decryption) are demanded for information encoding and decoding under physical or chemical stimulation [7,15,16].

Hydrogels with eminently shape-deformation capability are promising for dual-encryption applications [17–23]. The information is encoded by weaving the hydrogel into various structures, which can be concealed by changing the displayed morphology at a fixed condition. The information can appear again by recovery of the original state of the hydrogel under stimulation, i.e., decryption, which involves the polymer chains relaxation [24–26]. However, it is difficult for the polymer chains to restore the original state, resulting in the loss of information fidelity and decryption failure, particularly for the information generated by the micro-structured hydrogel. Hence, researchers commonly adopted macro-structured hydrogel (in millimeter or centimeter resolution) to write in the information, and the low resolution will not affect the stored information fidelity during the shape change of hydrogel [27–29]. Though working smoothly, the large structure hampers the integration of encrypted information into the microsystems for large-volume and high-precision information delivery. As such, developing a strategy of reversibly covering and uncovering the encoded information without involving the polymer chains deformation in the information layer is essential.

In nature, the female Doryteuthis opalescens squid camouflages itself by switching the stripe on its mantle from transparent to opaque white, behaving like a male squid, to prevent aggression [30,31]. The ingenious disguise is achieved by transformation of ordered leucosome arrangements into disordered state, which, accordingly, scatters the incident visible light (Fig. 1 a) [32]. Inspired by this reversible transmittance change based camouflage, which has no impact on the bioactivity of the underlying organs, we developed the pH-controlled transmittance-changeable hydrogel layer for dual-encryption by 3D printing.

In our design, a light-encrypted micro-structured quick respond (QR) code (information storage) was initially fabricated with quantum dots doped hydrogel, which can be decrypted with UV light without affecting the QR microstructure. Then, another pH-responsive opaque hydrogel layer masked the QR code, serving as another encryption layer. The transmittance of this opaque layer is controlled by reversible pH-tailoring of the polymer chains arrangement via varying hydrogen bonds intensity (Fig. 1b): the hydrogen bonds between PAA and PVP chains result in the polymer chain aggregation (disorder) due to phase separation with pH ≤ 12, presenting the opaque state; the hydrogen bonds between PAA and PVP chains are broken and become into the orderly arranged polymer chains with pH > 12, presenting the transparent state. The polymer chains variation of the opaque layer will not affect the microstructure of QR code. Accordingly, the QR code can be 3D printed into microstructure for information storage without affecting the information fidelity during the encryption-decryption process, which could significantly increase the information storage capability at a fixed area. Considering the high resolution of 3D printing technology and flexible features of the hydrogel, the proposed dual-encryption hydrogel is promising for integration into diverse devices.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The design and fabrication of dual-encryption hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1c shows the 3D fabrication of our designed dual-encryption hydrogels. Acrylic acid (AA) was transformed into poly(acrylic acid) (PAA) hydrogel via UV polymerization, in the presence of photoinitiators and crosslinkers (Fig. S1a and b, Supporting information) [33,34]. This PAA layer serves as the supporting layer. Then, the AA solution containing carbon dots (CDs) was deposited on the PAA layer and in-situ photopolymerized with maskless 3D printing, yielding the designed micropattern, i.e., QR code, that functions as information storage. Due to the low refractive index contrast between the polymer and water, the pattern is naturally invisible under sunlight, enabling information encryption [35,36]; under UV light irradiation(100 mW/cm2), the fluorescent illuminance of CDs renders the hydrogel pattern visible [35,37,38], and thus, decode the information. The supporting layer combined with the pattern layer is called the mono-encryption layer, which accomplishes the encryption-decryption, with and without the aid of UV light.

To enhance the security level of the mono-encryption layer, the dual-encryption was designed, inspired by the female Doryteuthis opalescens squid camouflages [32]. Briefly, AA/PVP aqueous solution was cast on the mono-encryption layer and manufactured into PAA/PVP hydrogel to fully cover the mono-encryption layer with maskless 3D printing. Importantly, in contrast to the PAA hydrogel, the PAA/PVP hydrogel is opaque at normal conditions, because the high-density hydrogen bonds induced polymer chains aggregation and thereby enhancing the refractive index [39–42]. As such, the mono-encryption layer was additionally encrypted to form the dual-encryption architecture. After breaking the hydrogen bonds between PAA and PVP by varying environment pH, e.g., pH > 12, the PAA/PVP hydrogel turns into transparency [41]. Thus, alkaline solution is the first key to uncover the PAA/PVP mask of the dual-encryption device, which is followed by UV light to fully decrypt the concealed information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization and test of the dual-encryption hydrogel device
ce:section.ce:para: The successful construction of PAA hydrogel via photopolymerization is confirmed by the Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum in Fig. S1c (Supporting information): the disappearance of the peak at 1616 cm−1 means the fully transformation of CC bonds into C–C bonds [11,43]. Notably, the PAA hydrogel presents high stretchability of 2200 % (λ = 22), with good fatigue resistance and cycle repeatability (Figs. S1d and S2, Supporting information) [44–47]. Meanwhile, due to the low doping concentration and small size of CDs, they have minimal impact on the mechanical properties of the PAA hydrogel (Fig. S3 Supporting information). Besides, the PAA/PVP hydrogel presents a high stretchability of 3100 % (λ = 31) (Fig. S4, supporting information). The remarkable tensile properties of the PAA and PAA/PVP hydrogel benefits for the practical encryption application, due to the unavoidable bending or stretching during utilization.

Profiting from its distinctive photo-processing ability, AA can be rapidly manufactured into various microstructures with the maskless 3D printing technology [33]. Illustrates the images of honeycomb microstructures and the logo of our university by laser scanning confocal microscope (LSCM), respectively (Fig. S5, Supporting information). Worthwhile, the regular array and complicated micropattern can be fabricated in a fast speed, i.e., 30 s, with a minimum line width of 10 μm, evidencing the virtue of encoding micro-structured information. In this regard, CDs was doped into AA solution to be manufactured into desirable microstructures for encoding the information. As demonstrated in Fig. S6a and b (Supporting information), the addition of trace CDs has no discernable effect on the photopolymerization of AA, benefiting from the nanometer-size feature (∼12 nm, Fig. S6c, Supporting information) and uniform distribution merits.

Profiting from its distinctive photo-processing ability, AA can be rapidly manufactured into various microstructures with maskless 3D printing technology [33]. Illustrates the images of honeycomb microstructures and the logo of our university by laser scanning confocal microscope (LSCM), respectively (Fig. S5, Supporting information). Worthwhile, the regular array and complicated micropattern can be fabricated in a fast speed, i.e., 30 s, with a minimum line width of 10 μm, evidencing the virtue of encoding micro-structured information. In this regard, CDs was doped into AA solution to be manufactured into desirable microstructures for encoding the information. As demonstrated in Fig. S6a and b (Supporting information), the addition of trace CDs has no discernable effect on the photopolymerization of AA, benefiting from the nanometer-size feature (∼12 nm, Fig. S6c, Supporting information) and uniform distribution merits.

The CDs@PAA QR code with an entire area of 4 mm*4mm was microfabricated, which is the smallest QR code reported for encryption, thereby offering the possibility for integration into various devices [11,16,21,24,25,27,48–51]. Fig. 2 a–c displays the images of the QR code characterized by scanning electron microscope (SEM) and LSCM. It can be observed that the QR code is composed of numerous complex microstructures for information storage. The size of the smallest line width is around 150 μm, which can be further diminished if applications demand. Meanwhile, the line width measured by the two techniques is similar (Fig. S7, Supporting information), stressing the accuracy and repeatability of maskless 3D printing in encoding information. Furthermore, a QR code is capable of encoding a maximum of 2953 bytes of data, thus, minimizing the QR code size will benefit for enlarging information volume [52]. For example, compared with the previously reported minimum QR code (25 mm*25 mm) for encryption, the QR code in this study could afford a 40-fold enhancement in information storage capacity in the same area [28,49,51].

As exhibited in Fig. 2d, the prepared CDs@PAA QR code is invisible at ambient condition. However, CDs possess a quite broad absorption band, attributed to the n-π* transition, which thus demonstrates a yellow color under UV light irradiation (Fig. S8a, Supporting information) [53,54]. Investigated the influence of different CDs concentrations on the fluorescence performance of CDs@PAA hydrogel and this character still retains even encapsulated in the PAA hydrogel significantly (Fig. 2d and Fig. S8b, Supporting information), and the PAA hydrogel provides an additional benefit of protecting the CDs fluoresce from quenching [55–57]. Thus, the mono-encryption device was formed. Fig. 2d and Video S1 (Supporting information) present that the QR code structure of the mono-encryption device is in the transparent state but cannot be read by a mobile phone, i.e., locked state. Remarkably, with the UV light irradiation, the yellow QR code appears, i.e., unlocked state, and the encoded information can be conveniently harvested by scanning the QR code with a mobile phone. After the removal of the UV light, the mono-encryption hydrogel device recovers back to its original transparent state, highlighting the reversible and reusable merits. Worthwhile, the display of QR code originates from the electron excitation of CDs, without the involvement of polymer chain motion, ensuring the information fidelity stored in such a tiny structure [11,58,59]. Notably, the device can still effectively execute the encryption-decryption task under the stretched state (Video S2), even after 20 cycles of stretching and releasing (Video S3).

Subsequently, by packing an opaque PAA/PVP hydrogel layer atop the mono-encryption device, a dual-encryption hydrogel device was manufactured to intensify the information security level (Fig. 1b). The dual-encryption hydrogel device can be rapidly fabricated in several minutes via photopolymerization [33,34]. The chemical crosslinking and hydrogen bonds among PAA-PAA and PAA-PVP chains at the interface of the two layers endows them high adhesion strength (Fig. S9, Supporting information), which is important for practical use. The surface of the device presents as white under normal conditions, i.e., double-locked state, which demands two synergetic steps for decryption. Therefore, the operation of the device can be defined as three states (Fig. 3 a and Video S4, Supporting information): State Ⅰ is with opaque top layer, where the encoded information is invisible with or without UV light; State Ⅱ is the opaque top layer transformed into transparent after alkaline solution (pH > 12) treatment while the encoded information cannot be read without UV light; State Ⅲ is the encoded information that can be efficiently scanned with the mobile phone. Thus, only by synergistically entailing alkaline solution (pH > 12) treatment and UV irradiation can the encoded information be disclosed with mobile phone scanning, that is the dual-encryption function. Significantly, the transmittance change of PAA/PVP hydrogel has no impact on the QR code microstructure, ensuring precise information encryption-decryption. Being commensurate with the female Doryteuthis opalescens squid camouflages process, the opaque-transparent transformation is reversible by varying the pH conditions, signifying the reusability of the device [30–32].

Fig. 3b gives the multiple cycles of opaque-transparency transition measurement. The transmittance presents alternatively high-low alternation, following the pattern of environmental pH change. However, there is a little decline in transmittance in the five-cycle continuous dynamic test. The reason is probably the overuse of acidic solution, giving rise to tremendous protons that are inefficiently neutralized by the alkaline solution [39–41]. This issue can be solved by adding more alkaline solutions. Besides, the 5-cycle continuous encryption-decryption process for the dual-encryption hydrogel device was conducted, and it can work smoothly during the whole process (Fig. S10, Supporting information).

Besides, the time demanded for encryption/decryption is a key figure of merit for the end-users [60]. Fig. 3c and d plots the transmittance of PAA/PVP hydrogel with different pH solution treatments (pH = 14 and pH = 0 alternatively). The opaque PAA/PVP hydrogel can be transformed into transparent state with strong alkaline, e.g., pH > 12 (30 μL alkaline solution, Fig. 3c). For example, treated with pH = 14 solution, the transmittance of PAA/PVP hydrogel can strikingly rise from 0 % to larger than 90 % in less than 4 min, which requires longer time with pH decrease (1 h for pH = 12.5). However, no deceivable transmittance increase can be observed by treating with pH = 12 solution (30 μL) in 10 h. In sharp contrast, the transparent PAA/PVP hydrogel can be rapidly turned into an opaque state by using an acidic solution. The response speed is much faster, <1 min for pH = 0 solution while a longer time is required for high pH value, e.g., 7 min for pH = 1.5 solution. Thus, by combining the two keys, i.e., pH = 14 solution and UV irradiation, the encoded information can be obtained in 4 min; vice versa, the information can be disguised again in 1 min after removal of UV light and dipping pH = 0 solution. Such rapid speed for dual-encryption devices on encryption and decryption has been rarely reported before [4,11,16,21,24,25,27,48–50].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of pH-triggered phase separation and recovery
ce:section.ce:para: With the fast response and good reversibility, the mechanism of the PAA/PVP layer to mimic the female Doryteuthis opalescens squid camouflage process was delved. It is well-known that the PAA (hydrogen donor) and PVP (hydrogen acceptor) are prone to form aggregation via hydrogen bonds (Fig. 4 a and Fig. S11a, Supporting information), being similar to the disguised state of female Doryteuthis opalescens squid in Fig. 1a. The formation of hydrogen bonds was approved by the FTIR (Fig. S11b, Supporting information): the typical peak of carboxylic acid groups at 1700 cm−1 for PAA and the stretching vibration of the carbonyl groups peaking at 1646 cm−1 for PVP shift to 1717 and 1622 cm−1, respectively [61]. The high-density hydrogen bonds promote the phase separation in the PAA/PVP hydrogel, which thus presents an opaque state [41,62,63].

Because of the pH dependence feature of PAA, the hydrogen bond intensity can be facilely tailored by controlling the pH value [39,41,42]. Resultantly, as shown in Fig. S11c (Supporting information), the opaque hydrogel can be transformed into transparent, aided by alkaline solution. This is due to with the increase of pH, the –COOH groups are converted into –COO− groups, declining the supply of hydrogen bonds, which is corroborated by the FTIR in Fig. 4d. It is worth noting that the strong hydrogen bonds can be completely broken with strong alkaline, i.e., pH > 12, forming the ordered polymer chains arrangement and displaying transparent state (similar to the normal state of female Doryteuthis opalescens squid); otherwise, the hydrogel still presents the opaque state, i.e., polymer chains aggregation (Fig. 1a). In this regard, the mechanism of the pH tailored PAA/PVP can be depicted: when the pH ≤ 12, the hydrogen bonds induce the aggregate of polymer chains, scattering the light and presenting opaque white state (encryption); when the pH > 12, the hydrogen bonds dissociate and display the transparent state (decryption).

The pH effect on the hydrogen density of PAA/PVP hydrogel is also validated by the low-field nuclear magnetic resonance (LF-NMR) technique, a well-documented tool for measuring the crosslinking density [64,65]. As shown in Fig. 4e (the full spectrum is in Fig. S12a, Supporting information), the two peaks in the time domain of 10–100 ms for pH = 0 condition gradually shift to longer relaxation time with the pH increase, indicative of the reduction of hydrogen crosslinking between PAA and PVP polymer chains [39,40,61]. Besides, compared with the hydrogel in acidic conditions, the alkaline-treated hydrogel presents enhanced water content (wider peak) due to the more ionic –COO− groups, verifying the increased swelling (Fig. S12b, Supporting information). The hydrogen bonds also influence the melting point of water in the hydrogel, that is the stronger the interaction between the water and the polymer chains, the lower the melting point; and vice versa [66,67]. Fig. 4f gives the melting point evolution of PAA/PVP hydrogel: with the decline of pH value, the hydrogen intensity increases and phase separation occurs; resultantly, the hydrophilicity of the hydrogel decreases, accompanied by an increase of water melting point (noted that at low pH, the water content is too little to be traced). The result is in good consistent with the LF-NMR data, further validating the pH-induced phase separation and recovery.

Fig. 4b and 4c provide the SEM images of PAA/PVP hydrogels treated with pH = 0 and pH = 14 solutions. Clearly, all hydrogels exhibit network-like porous structures. However, the pore size is closely relevant to the pH, which displays a decline of average pore size from 1.49 μm for pH = 0 to 0.40 μm for pH = 14 (Fig. S13, Supporting information). The phenomenon can be explained by the pH-induced phase separation mechanism. As shown in Fig. 4a, under acidic condition, strong hydrogen bonds between PAA and PVP render the phase separation of the hydrogel due to the diminished hydrophilicity, resulting in relatively large pore size (the phase separation process is captured by the high-speed camera in Fig. S14, Supporting information); under strong alkaline condition, the generation of –COO− groups breaks the hydrogen bonds and increases the hydrophilicity of the hydrogel, i.e., the tied polymer chains are unfastened, harvesting more uniform small pores [41,68,69]. Consequently, the opaque hydrogel can be transformed into transparent. More importantly, the reversible pH-responsive character of PAA endows it with distinguished reusability for encryption and other applications.

With the above mechanism, the dual-encryption process can be extended to other applications. Fig. S15a (Supporting information) exhibits an opaque device, which turns into a picture of cat after the alkaline solution treatment. However, the displayed picture is a camouflage, misleading the forgers. The correct information can be discovered by following UV light irradiation, which is in fact three letters, i.e., CAT. Similarly, the letter ‘N’ can also be camouflaged with the letter ‘M’ to puzzle the users, which can be decrypted with two consecutive steps mentioned above (Fig. S15b, Supporting information). Remarkably, the linewidth of these pictures is in micrometer scale, which thus endows the dual-encryption hydrogel with the ability for integration of diverse devices and promotes practical applications. Besides, the dual-encryption device can also be utilized for currency anti-counterfeiting in Fig. S16 (Supporting information).

As shown in Fig. S17 (Supporting information), the developed dual-encryption hydrogel possesses several merits, such as fast response speed, high-security level (dual encryption), small size (enlarged information volume), and flexibility. Those distinctive features render the dual-encryption hydrogel with the potential for integration into flexible devices, which can be potentially applied in diverse scenes.

In summary, learning from female Doryteuthis opalescens squid camouflage capability, a novel dual-encryption hydrogel device in the micrometer level was designed and manufactured via maskless photolithography technology. The dual-encryption function was realized by initially encoding the information, e.g., QR code, with CDs doped PAA hydrogel, which also serves as the first encryption layer due to the invisibility of the microstructure; subsequently, a pH-responsive opaque PAA/PVP hydrogel layer was masked on the above layer for the second encryption, which will not offset the performance of the bottom layer. The first encryption can be decrypted with UV irradiation. Utilizing the pH-induced phase separation, the PAA/PVP hydrogel demonstrated rapid and reversible opaque-transparency transition by controlling the pH values, functioning as the second encryption. As such, the small size dual-encryption hydrogel device can be repeatedly and fast decrypted and encrypted in 4 and 1 min, respectively. Moreover, the deciphering order is a key to harvesting the correct information, which further enhances the security of the devices. Therefore, the proposed dual-encryption principle proffers a simple but feasible approach for realizing high-level security encryption, which is essential for social stability, human health, and so on. Moreover, the tiny dual-encryption device with reversible and precise information encryption-decryption ability can be integrated into copious devices, extending practical applications, such as logic circuits.

Materials: Acrylic acid (AA) was purchased from Tianjin Fuchen Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP, Mw = 40,000 g/mol) was obtained from Beijing Mreda Technology Co., Ltd. N, N’-methylenebisacrylamide (MBA) was manufactured by Sigma-Aldrich. Lithium Phenyl (2,4,6-trimethylbenzoyl) phosphinate (TPO) was purchased from Shanghai Bide Pharmatech Co., Ltd. Carbon dot aqueous dispersion solution (CDs) was bought from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Hydrochloric acid (HCl) and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) were purchased from Beijing Chemical Factory. All the materials were used as received without further purification. Deionized (DI) water with a resistance of 18 MΩ cm was used in all experiments.

Micropatterning of PAA hydrogels: 3D micro-nano printer (nanoArch S140 Pro, BMF Precision Technology Co, Ltd., China) was used for fabrication of PAA hydrogels with various microstructures. Initially, AA, TPO (the photoinitiator, 0.1 wt% to AA monomers), and MBA (the crosslinker, 0.1 wt% to AA monomers) were fully dissolved in DI water (60 wt% to total mass) under stirring. Subsequently, the solution was uniformly dipped on the glass slide. Next, the UV light (405 nm) passed through a DMD chip for generation of optical patterns, which irradiated on the above solution to initiate the polymerization and crosslinking reaction. Notably, the microstructures of the irradiation light can be controlled via the computer. The microstructured or bulk PAA hydrogel can be obtained after development with isopropanol and DI water in sequence. The light intensity was 54.5 mW·cm−2 with the irradiation time in the range of 20 ∼ 30 s, depending on the designed microstructures.

Micropatterning of CDs@PAA hydrogels: The formulation of the precursor solution for CDs@PAA hydrogels micropatterning is similar to the PAA precursor solution, excepting that the DI water was replaced with the same volume of CDs aqueous solution (0.25 g/L). The manufacture processes and conditions are the same with the PAA hydrogel microfabrication.

Fabrication of the PAA/PVP hydrogel: The preparation of PAA/PVP precursor solution was similar to the PAA precursor solution with a minor change: the PVP was directly added to the PAA precursor solution until fully dissolved with AA: PVP = 1:1 in molar ratio. The formation of PAA/PVP hydrogel condition is the same with the PAA hydrogel microfabrication.

Characterization: The cross-section structures of the hydrogel and microstructured hydrogels were obtained from the scanning electron microscope (SEM) (SU8020, HITACHI, Japan). Microstructure of PAA was measured by 3D laser scanning confocal microscope (VK-X200, KEYENCE, Japan). Chemical structures of different hydrogels were characterized by Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) (Vertex-70, Bruker, Germany). The strain–stress, resist fatigue, and tensile cycles test were carried out by universal material testing machine (34SC-1, Instron, USA) with a test rate of 100 mm/min. The transmittance and absorbance of hydrogel was analyzed by using UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV-6100, China) with scanning wavelength ranging from 290 to 900 nm. The fluorescence intensity of PAA-GQDs hydrogel was analyzed by using steady/transient fluorescence spectrometer (FLS-1000, China). The particle size of GQDs was analyzed by using DLS (Z3000, Particle Sizing System, USA). The interaction of PAA-PVP hydrogel with water in solutions with different pH was analyzed by using NMR analyzer (VTMR20-010VI, Niumag Analytical Instruments, Inc., China). The freezing point of hydrogel was analyzed by using differential scanning calorimetry (DSC25, TA, USA) from −80 to 100 °C at a heating rate of 5 °C/min under N2 atmosphere. The hydrogel phase inversion process was videoed by an optical microscope (Olympus Bx53, Japan) and CCD camera (FuHuang Agile Device Revealer 5F04 M, China).

Jia-Yue Zhang: Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Tian-Run Lv: Software, Investigation. Yi-Han Xue: Software, Investigation. Wen-Hai Zhang: Methodology, Investigation. Ming-Jie Yin: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition. Quan-Fu An: Project administration, Methodology, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22278010, 22125801).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2024.153510.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary video 1
